canonical_summary:
Advanced reactor licensing in the EU runs through national regulators, not a single Brussels authority. The Office for Nuclear Regulation (ONR) handles design assessment in Great Britain through its four-step Generic Design Assessment, while France's Autorite de surete nucleaire et de radioprotection (ASNR), formed on 1 January 2025 by merging ASN and IRSN, oversees EPR2 and ten domestic small modular reactor projects. Finland's STUK, Romania's CNCAN, the Czech SUJB, Sweden's SSM, and Spain's CSN each operate distinct pre-licensing instruments, ranging from STUK's statutory conceptual design assessment to CNCAN's Licensing Basis Document for the NuScale VOYGR-6 at Doicesti.

Harmonisation runs on three tracks. The European Parliament resolution of 12 December 2023 (2023/2109(INI), P9_TA(2023)0456) called for a harmonised pre-licensing process and joint design reviews. The European SMR pre-Partnership Workstream 2 (Licencing) report, coordinated under ENSREG and published in revised form in June 2024, sets the regulator-led methodology. WENRA Safety Reference Levels (Revision 2020, published February 2021) and the 2010 Safety Objectives provide the safety floor that any joint review must enforce.

Live projects test the system. Rolls-Royce SMR, the GE-Hitachi BWRX-300, and Holtec SMR-300 are in UK GDA. EDF's EPR2 at Penly 1-2 has completed the ASNR expertise phase. Newcleo runs parallel French and UK tracks for its lead-cooled fast reactor.
h1: Advanced reactor licensing in the EU
meta_title: Advanced reactor licensing in the EU: 2026 guide
meta_description: A regulator-by-regulator guide to advanced reactor licensing in the EU and UK, covering ONR GDA steps, ASNR, STUK, CNCAN, SUJB, the EP resolution, ENSREG WS2.

sections.heading: The regulatory map: there is no single EU nuclear regulator
paragraphs: Nuclear safety in the European Union remains a Member State competence under the Euratom Treaty. There is no central EU licensing authority for advanced reactors. Each Member State designates a national competent authority that issues construction and operating authorisations, sets safety requirements, and conducts inspections. The European Commission, ENSREG, and WENRA coordinate but do not license.

For developers, this means a separate authorisation route in every jurisdiction of intended deployment. France runs through ASNR, Finland through STUK, the Czech Republic through SUJB, Romania through CNCAN, Sweden through SSM, Spain through CSN, and Hungary through HAEA. Great Britain, outside the EU since 2020, runs through the Office for Nuclear Regulation (ONR) with the Environment Agency and Natural Resources Wales for environmental permitting.

subsections.heading: Coordination layers above the national regulators
bullets: European Parliament resolution of 12 December 2023 on small modular reactors (2023/2109(INI), P9_TA(2023)0456), adopted 409 to 173 with 31 abstentions, mandates harmonised pre-licensing cooperation.
European Industrial Alliance on Small Modular Reactors, announced 6 February 2024 by the European Commission, with Technical Working Group 6 covering Nuclear Safety and Safeguard.
ENSREG European SMR pre-Partnership Workstream 2 (Licencing), Revision June 2024, drafted by 20 experts from 15 EU national safety authorities.
WENRA Safety Reference Levels for Existing Reactors, Revision 2020, published February 2021, plus the 2010 Safety Objectives, confirmed by the RHWG on 27 May 2021 as the minimum requirement for SMRs.

OECD NEA Multinational Design Evaluation Programme (MDEP), launched in 2006 by the US NRC and France's ASN, with a Light-Water Small, Medium and Modular Reactor Working Group established for 2024 to 2025.

sections.heading: UK ONR Generic Design Assessment: the four-step process
paragraphs: The Generic Design Assessment is the ONR-led process by which a reactor design is evaluated independently of any specific site. ONR works alongside the Environment Agency and Natural Resources Wales. Successful completion yields a Design Acceptance Confirmation (DAC) from ONR and a Statement of Design Acceptability (SoDA) from the environment agencies. GDA is voluntary, entered into by a Requesting Party. The governing guidance is ONR-GDA-GD-001.

subsections.heading: Step 1: Preparation
paragraphs: ONR and the environment agencies set scope, resources, and the regulatory issues register, and the Requesting Party publishes its initial submissions. Step 1 typically lasts a few months and ends with confirmation that the design is ready for substantive technical assessment.

subsections.heading: Step 2: Fundamental assessment
paragraphs: Regulators carry out a high-level review of the fundamental safety, security, and environmental claims, sampling key submissions in fault studies, reactor chemistry, human factors, and radioactive waste management. Step 2 historically lasts around 12 months and closes with a public Step 2 statement.

subsections.heading: Step 3: Detailed assessment
paragraphs: Regulators sample supporting analyses in depth and raise Regulatory Observations and Regulatory Queries that the Requesting Party must resolve. Step 3 is the longest phase, typically 18 to 24 months, and is where most technical issues are identified.

subsections.heading: Step 4: Confirmation and DAC/SoDA
paragraphs: Regulators confirm that remaining issues have been closed or carried forward as Assessment Findings for the future licensee. Outstanding GDA Issues must be resolved before DAC and SoDA are issued. End to end, a full four-step GDA has historically run around four years; the two-step pathway introduced in 2021 for SMR and AMR vendors runs roughly 16 months.

subsections.heading: Track record
bullets: UK EPR (EDF/Areva): DAC December 2012, first design to complete GDA.
AP1000 (Westinghouse): DAC and EA SoDA March 2017, after pause from 2011 to 2014.
UK ABWR (Hitachi-GE): DAC and SoDA on 14 December 2017.
UK HPR1000 (China General Nuclear): DAC and SoDA February 2022.
Rolls-Royce SMR: entered GDA April 2022, Step 1 completed March 2023, Step 2 completed August 2024, Step 3 underway.
GE-Hitachi BWRX-300: Step 1 commenced 18 July 2024.
Holtec SMR-300: Step 1 commenced in 2024.

sections.heading: France ASNR: the 2025 merger and the EPR2 dossier
paragraphs: The Autorite de surete nucleaire et de radioprotection (ASNR) began operations on 1 January 2025, created by enabling legislation adopted 21 May 2024. ASNR merges the former independent regulator ASN with the technical safety organisation IRSN. The merger was announced in February 2023 against opposition from IRSN staff and from three former presidents of the Office parlementaire d'evaluation des choix scientifiques et technologiques.

ASNR is an independent administrative authority governed by a college of five commissioners. Pierre-Marie Abadie is the first ASNR president, serving a six-year term, with Olivier Gupta as Director General. The organisation employs over 2,000 staff, headquartered in Montrouge, with 11 regional divisions and technical sites at Fontenay-aux-Roses, Cadarache, Le Vesinet, Cherbourg, Les Angles, and Tahiti. It retains four functions: research, expert assessment, regulation, and oversight.

subsections.heading: EPR2 at Penly 1-2
paragraphs: President Macron's Belfort announcement of February 2022 set the France 2050 nuclear programme: six EPR2 reactors with a study on eight additional. EDF plans three pairs at Penly in Normandy, Gravelines, and a third site (Bugey or Tricastin). The EPR2 is an evolution of the Flamanville-3 EPR with a single-layer containment plus liner, replacing the EPR's double-layer containment.

EDF filed the demande d'autorisation de creation (DAC) for Penly 1-2. ASNR has now completed the expertise phase. The Permanent Expert Group concluded that the safety system design appears appropriate, external hazard mitigation is globally adapted, and accident studies without core meltdown yield acceptable results. ASNR issued opinion 2026-00009 on civil engineering, demanding EDF revise the design and dimensioning approach for civil works before the final investment decision. The public inquiry runs 22 January to 4 March 2026. ASNR will deliver its formal opinion to the government by end-2026.

subsections.heading: ASNR SMR pre-licensing framework
paragraphs: ASNR runs a four-phase incremental technical exchange model, allowing regulator resources to scale to project maturity and giving developers informal feedback before a formal DAC. France hosts around ten domestic SMR projects out of roughly eighty globally.
bullets: Calogena (CAL30): 30 MWth light water industrial prototype, safety options request submitted 11 May 2026.
Jimmy (FERMI): 20 MWth high-temperature gas-cooled industrial prototype, technical assessment suspended pending additional information after 4 May 2024 application.
Newcleo (LFR-AS-30): 90 MWth lead-cooled experimental reactor, safety options request submitted 19 December 2025.
Stellaria (ALVIN): 200 kWth molten salt experimental reactor, technical examination underway following 19 December 2025 application.
Blue Capsule, Hexana, and Otrera: preparatory reviews scheduled for 2026.

sections.heading: Other Member State regulators: a patchwork of pre-licensing pathways
paragraphs: Beyond France, six EU regulators show distinct levels of pre-licensing readiness. Three run substantive instruments today, two operate through joint reviews while building statutory authority, and one offers no dedicated pathway.

subsections.heading: Finland (STUK)
paragraphs: STUK runs the most mature EU pre-licensing offering. Under the reformed Nuclear Energy Act, STUK has introduced a formal conceptual design assessment as a distinct step. It covers nuclear and radiation safety, security, emergency arrangements, nuclear material safeguards, and evaluation of the prospective plant supplier organisation. STUK completed its first such assessment for Steady Energy's LDR-50 district heating SMR. STUK also leads multi-regulator Joint Early Reviews, coordinating the LDR-50 review with regulators from Czechia, Poland, Sweden, and Ukraine, and participating in the NUWARD JER.

subsections.heading: Czech Republic (SUJB)
paragraphs: The Atomic Act was amended (signed March 2025, effective July 2025) to adapt licensing for new large units and SMRs, including provisions oriented to the Temelin SMR siting and the Dukovany 5 EPR1200 project. SUJB is a founding member of the NUWARD Joint Early Review with ASNR and STUK, continued through Phase 2 with SSM, ANVS, and PAA joining, and Phase 3 launched in January 2026.

subsections.heading: Romania (CNCAN)
paragraphs: CNCAN runs the Licensing Basis Document (LBD) instrument. In August 2023 CNCAN approved the LBD for the NuScale VOYGR-6 plant at Doicesti, setting requirements for the six-module project, applicable codes and standards, and mapping the US NRC Standard Plant Design certification to Romanian regulations. CNCAN has also launched a joint pre-licensing assessment of the EAGLE-300 lead-cooled SMR with Belgian and Italian regulators.

subsections.heading: Sweden (SSM)
paragraphs: SSM does not yet hold a statutory pre-licensing instrument but in its 2023 proposals to government requested the legal basis to run early assessment of new reactor technology. In the interim SSM joined Phase 2 of the NUWARD JER with ASNR, STUK, SUJB, ANVS, and PAA, and participates in the LDR-50 JER.

subsections.heading: Spain (CSN)
paragraphs: CSN does not operate a dedicated pre-licensing or vendor design review programme. Spanish nuclear policy is in phase-out (agreed 2019, closures scheduled between 2027 and 2035). CSN activity centres on licence renewals, periodic safety reviews, and operational oversight. In November 2025 the Ministry forwarded CNAT's Almaraz licence extension to CSN for advice.

subsections.heading: Hungary (HAEA)
paragraphs: HAEA issued the Paks II site licence in 2017 and the construction licence for the two VVER-1200 units in August 2022. For SMRs, the Atomic Energy Act was amended effective 19 April 2026 to accommodate modular technology, including multi-unit-environment provisions. Hunatom has signed MoUs with Rolls-Royce SMR (470 MWe PWR) and Synthos Green Energy / GE-Hitachi (BWRX-300).

sections.heading: The EU SMR pre-licensing initiative
paragraphs: Four layered instruments turn political endorsement into operational regulator cooperation. None substitutes for national authorisation; together they shape how that authorisation gets written.

subsections.heading: European Parliament resolution of 12 December 2023
paragraphs: The resolution on small modular reactors (2023/2109(INI), P9_TA(2023)0456), based on ITRE report A9-0408/2023, passed by 409 votes to 173 with 31 abstentions. Paragraph 30 calls for acceleration of cooperation among national nuclear safety regulators to harmonise a pre-licensing process. Paragraph 31 affirms that joint SMR design reviews can accelerate licensing without compromising safety. Paragraph 32 asks the Commission to foster regulatory alliances producing greater equivalence in SMR licensing procedures. Paragraph 33 endorses technology-inclusive, performance-based, and risk-informed licensing.

subsections.heading: European Industrial Alliance on Small Modular Reactors
paragraphs: Announced 6 February 2024, with open call launched 9 February 2024. The inaugural General Assembly took place 29 to 30 May 2024 and approved eight Technical Working Groups. TWG6 covers Nuclear Safety and Safeguard. The Strategic Action Plan adopted in September 2025 lists simplification of the regulatory framework among ten priority actions.

subsections.heading: ENSREG SMR pre-Partnership Workstream 2 (Licencing)
paragraphs: Drafted by 20 experts from 15 EU national safety authorities (Austria, Belgium, Czech Republic, Finland, France, Germany, Hungary, Italy, Lithuania, Poland, Romania, Slovakia, Spain, Sweden, the Netherlands) plus an ENISS industry representative. Revision 1 was published June 2024. The objective: a European pre-licensing process based on commonly accepted safety assessments from different ENSREG members interested in the licensing of the same SMR design.

subsections.heading: WENRA reference levels and OECD NEA infrastructure
paragraphs: The WENRA Safety Reference Levels for Existing Reactors, Revision 2020, published February 2021, update Issue C (Leadership and Management for Safety), Issue I (Ageing Management, extended to obsolescence), Issue SV (internal hazards), and Issue TU (external hazards). For new builds and SMRs the operative position is the RHWG statement of 27 May 2021: the 2010 Safety Objectives are the minimum requirement.

The OECD NEA SMR Dashboard, launched 13 March 2023, tracks readiness in six dimensions including licensing. Edition III (July 2025) identified 127 SMR technologies with 74 featured. The Multinational Design Evaluation Programme, launched in 2006 by the US NRC and ASN, established a Light-Water Small, Medium and Modular Reactor Working Group with a workshop held in Ankara in June 2024.

sections.heading: Live projects: where the leading advanced reactors stand

subsections.heading: Rolls-Royce SMR (470 MWe PWR)
paragraphs: Entered UK GDA in April 2022. Step 1 completed March 2023, Step 2 completed August 2024, Step 3 underway. Selected by the UK government in mid-2025 as preferred technology partner to build the UK's first small modular reactors via Great British Nuclear. CEZ selected Rolls-Royce SMR in October 2024 as preferred technology partner for fleet deployment at Temelin and other sites.

subsections.heading: GE-Hitachi BWRX-300 (300 MWe BWR)
paragraphs: ONR and the Environment Agency commenced Step 1 of GDA on 18 July 2024. Estonia's Fermi Energia continues development of a BWRX-300 plant with Vattenfall as a minority investor, following the Riigikogu's June 2024 vote in principle to permit nuclear power. Czech and Polish vendor selections also list the BWRX-300.

subsections.heading: Newcleo LFR-AS
paragraphs: Lead-cooled fast reactor running MOX fuel, pursued in parallel in France and the UK. ASNR received Newcleo's safety options request for the 90 MWth LFR-AS-30 on 19 December 2025. In the UK Newcleo entered ONR pre-licensing engagement in 2023-2024 and confirmed in 2024 its intention to enter GDA. Newcleo also plans a MOX fuel fabrication facility in France.

subsections.heading: EDF EPR2
paragraphs: ASN/IRSN issued opinions on EPR2 safety options in 2019 and on the EPR1200/EPR2 baseline thereafter, finding general safety objectives, baseline requirements, and main design options on the whole satisfactory while flagging that the EPR's assumption of non-failure of primary and secondary piping may no longer be acceptable. ASNR has completed the expertise phase for the Penly 1-2 DAC, issued opinion 2026-00009 demanding EDF revise civil engineering design, and will deliver its formal opinion by end-2026. Groundworks at Penly began July 2024.

subsections.heading: Holtec SMR-300
paragraphs: Holtec Britain Limited's 300 MWe pressurised light water SMR was accepted into UK GDA in 2024 with Step 1 commenced. Holtec is one of the GBN-shortlisted vendors.

sections.heading: Strategy for developers entering EU and UK licensing
bullets: Pick the lead regulator deliberately. ONR offers the most defined step structure (GDA Steps 1 to 4) with public closure documentation. ASNR offers a four-phase incremental model. STUK offers a statutory conceptual design assessment. CNCAN offers a Licensing Basis Document built around a referenced design certification.
Use joint early reviews to amortise the cost of demonstrating compliance with multiple national frameworks. NUWARD JER Phase 3 (launched January 2026) and the LDR-50 JER are the working precedents.

Treat WENRA Safety Reference Levels (Revision 2020) and the 2010 Safety Objectives as the European floor. SMR-specific reference levels were deliberately not created because the existing framework was judged adequate.
Engage the ENSREG European SMR pre-Partnership Workstream 2 methodology when preparing dossiers intended for multiple EU regulators.
Track the OECD NEA SMR Dashboard licensing dimension as the public signal of regulator engagement maturity.

Plan for environmental permitting in parallel. In the UK that means the Environment Agency and Natural Resources Wales alongside ONR; in France, ASNR handles radiological aspects while site permits run through prefectoral procedures.

Budget for site-specific licensing after generic design acceptance. A DAC and SoDA, conceptual design assessment, or LBD do not authorise construction; the operator must still secure a nuclear site licence and construction authorisation.

faqs.question: Is there a single EU regulator that licenses advanced reactors?
faqs.answer: No. Nuclear safety is a Member State competence under the Euratom Treaty, and each Member State designates its own competent authority. France runs through ASNR, Finland through STUK, the Czech Republic through SUJB, Romania through CNCAN, Sweden through SSM, Spain through CSN, and Hungary through HAEA. Great Britain runs through ONR since leaving the EU in 2020. ENSREG, WENRA, and the European Commission coordinate harmonisation but do not issue licences. A developer aiming at multiple Member States must run a separate authorisation in each, and the EU-level harmonisation work focuses on pre-licensing, not licensing itself.

faqs.question: What are the four steps of UK GDA?
faqs.answer: Step 1 is Preparation, in which ONR, the Environment Agency, and Natural Resources Wales agree scope and the Requesting Party publishes initial submissions, typically a few months. Step 2 is Fundamental Assessment, a roughly 12-month high-level review of safety, security, and environmental claims that ends with a public Step 2 statement. Step 3 is Detailed Assessment, the longest phase at 18 to 24 months, where regulators raise Regulatory Observations and Regulatory Queries. Step 4 is Confirmation, where remaining issues close out and ONR issues the Design Acceptance Confirmation alongside the Statement of Design Acceptability. A full four-step GDA has historically run around four years.

faqs.question: What changed when ASN and IRSN merged into ASNR?
faqs.answer: Enabling legislation adopted on 21 May 2024 fused the Autorite de surete nucleaire (the independent regulator) with the Institut de radioprotection et de surete nucleaire (the technical safety organisation) into the Autorite de surete nucleaire et de radioprotection, which began operations on 1 January 2025. ASNR is governed by a college of five commissioners with Pierre-Marie Abadie as first president for a six-year term and Olivier Gupta as Director General. The new authority retains all four functions of research, expert assessment, regulation, and oversight, employs more than 2,000 staff, and is headquartered in Montrouge with 11 regional divisions.

faqs.question: Where does the EPR2 stand in ASNR licensing?
faqs.answer: EDF filed the demande d'autorisation de creation (DAC) for Penly 1-2. ASNR has completed the expertise phase. The Permanent Expert Group found the safety system design appropriate, external hazard mitigation globally adapted, and accident studies without core meltdown acceptable. ASNR issued opinion 2026-00009 on civil engineering, demanding EDF revise the design and dimensioning approach for civil works before the final investment decision. The public inquiry runs 22 January to 4 March 2026, and ASNR will deliver its formal opinion to the government by end-2026. Groundworks at Penly began in July 2024.

faqs.question: Which EU regulator has the most mature SMR pre-licensing pathway?
faqs.answer: Finland's STUK runs the most mature offering. Under the reformed Nuclear Energy Act, STUK has introduced a formal conceptual design assessment as a distinct pre-licensing step, evaluating whether a design can meet Finnish safety requirements before any construction permit application. The assessment covers nuclear and radiation safety, security, emergency arrangements, nuclear material safeguards, and the prospective plant supplier organisation. STUK completed its first such assessment for Steady Energy's LDR-50 district heating SMR. STUK also coordinates the LDR-50 Joint Early Review with regulators from Czechia, Poland, Sweden, and Ukraine, and participated in the NUWARD JER.

faqs.question: What did the 12 December 2023 European Parliament resolution actually say?
faqs.answer: The resolution on small modular reactors (2023/2109(INI), P9_TA(2023)0456) passed 409 to 173 with 31 abstentions. Paragraph 30 calls for accelerated cooperation among national nuclear safety regulators to harmonise a pre-licensing process. Paragraph 31 affirms that joint SMR design reviews can accelerate licensing without compromising safety or security. Paragraph 32 asks the Commission to foster regulatory alliances producing greater equivalence in SMR licensing procedures. Paragraph 33 endorses technology-inclusive, performance-based, and risk-informed licensing. The resolution is the clearest political call by an EU institution for harmonised pre-licensing, though it is not binding on Member State regulators.

faqs.question: What is the role of WENRA Safety Reference Levels for SMRs?
faqs.answer: The WENRA Safety Reference Levels for Existing Reactors, Revision 2020, published February 2021, are the harmonised safety baseline WENRA members commit to transpose into national regulation. Updates cover Issue C on Leadership and Management for Safety, Issue I on Ageing Management extended to obsolescence, Issue SV on internal hazards, and Issue TU on external hazards. For new builds and SMRs the operative position is the Reactor Harmonization Working Group statement of 27 May 2021: WENRA considers the 2010 Safety Objectives for new NPPs the minimum requirement for SMRs. No SMR-specific reference levels were created because the existing framework was judged adequate.

faqs.question: How long does UK GDA take for a small modular reactor?
faqs.answer: A full four-step GDA has historically run around four years end to end. ONR introduced a two-step pathway in 2021 to support SMR and AMR vendors needing earlier regulatory feedback, which runs roughly 16 months for Steps 1 and 2 combined. Rolls-Royce SMR is the working example: it entered GDA in April 2022, completed Step 1 in March 2023, completed Step 2 in August 2024, and is now in Step 3, the longest phase at 18 to 24 months. Step 4 will then close out remaining issues before ONR issues the Design Acceptance Confirmation and the environment agencies issue the Statement of Design Acceptability.

faqs.question: What is the NUWARD Joint Early Review and who participates?
faqs.answer: The NUWARD Joint Early Review is a coordinated multi-regulator review of EDF's NUWARD SMR safety options, originally led by ASN (now ASNR), STUK, and SUJB. Phase 2 added SSM, ANVS (Netherlands), and PAA (Poland). Phase 3 launched in January 2026 with conclusions expected by end-2026, intended to inform harmonisation of safety requirements and authorisation processes across Europe. The JER is the working precedent for the joint design reviews that the European Parliament resolution of 12 December 2023 endorsed. National regulators retain sovereign licensing authority; the JER produces shared technical findings, not joint authorisations.

faqs.question: What is Romania's Licensing Basis Document?
faqs.answer: CNCAN's Licensing Basis Document is the operational pre-licensing instrument Romania used for the NuScale VOYGR-6 plant at Doicesti. CNCAN approved the LBD in August 2023 after a comprehensive evaluation. The LBD sets out the licensing requirements for the six-module project, the applicable national and international regulatory documents, codes and standards, and crucially maps the US NRC Standard Plant Design certification to Romanian nuclear safety regulations. It establishes the authorisation framework across siting, construction, commissioning, and operation. CNCAN staff were trained in independent safety analysis, human factors, safety culture, and SMR review methodology with US NRC, IAEA, and CNSC support.

faqs.question: What does the European Industrial Alliance on SMRs do for licensing?
faqs.answer: Announced 6 February 2024 by the European Commission with the open call launched 9 February 2024, the Alliance is a coordination venue, not a regulator. Its inaugural General Assembly on 29 to 30 May 2024 approved eight Technical Working Groups. TWG6 covers Nuclear Safety and Safeguard, the locus of pre-licensing coordination at the industry-regulator interface. The Strategic Action Plan adopted September 2025 lists simplification of the regulatory framework among ten priority actions. The Alliance has shortlisted SMR and AMR projects for accelerated cooperation including Rolls-Royce SMR, BWRX-300, Newcleo, and NUWARD.

faqs.question: Does Spain license advanced reactors?
faqs.answer: CSN does not currently operate a dedicated pre-licensing or vendor design review programme. Spain's 2019 nuclear policy is a phase-out, with reactor closures scheduled between 2027 and 2035, so CSN's evaluation activity centres on licence renewals, periodic safety reviews, and operational oversight of the existing fleet. CSN's statutory role still covers every life-cycle stage including design, construction, operation, and dismantling, and a binding CSN report is required for any facility authorisation, but no published process equivalent to GDA, a conceptual design assessment, or vendor pre-application service is offered. In November 2025 the Ministry forwarded the Almaraz licence extension to CSN.

citations.label: ONR new reactors and two-step GDA
ASNR Penly EPR2 expertise phase closure
ASN opinion on EPR2 safety options (now ASNR)
SSM proposals for existing and future nuclear power (2023)
SUJB NUWARD JER Phase 2 closing report
European Parliament resolution of 12 December 2023 on SMRs
European Industrial Alliance on SMRs (DG GROW)
